> But I do think it's important to admit that a fertilized egg is a human life


Yes it's a form of human life. But so too is a removed kidney a form of human life. I get that an embryo will become a human being, that does not automatically mean it is currently a human being. That's the point of contention. We are all everyday in the process of dying. That does not mean we are today currently dead with the protections and rights of a corpse. We apply distinction primarily according to current state. I appreciate that an embryo is the formation of human life - I think the moral magnitude of this is massively overstated by equating initial formation of human life to immediate formation of a human person with human self realization and protection of a solitary human. A seed isn't a tree, an egg isn't a bird and there's no reason by the same equivalence that an embryo is already a full human person immediately from moment of formation. That doesn't align with how any of us view life otherwise - with primarily a present state evaluation.


> Many of the arguments that support abortion would also support infanticide ("it can't live on its own"), or killing disabled or homeless or even sleeping people ("lack consciousness").


There are over the top arguments which go too far into absurdity sure. Just like I am complaining about regarding calling early stage abortions infanticide. That's not specific to the right, yes people on the left make equally ridiculous arguments as well.


But what you're saying here first of all is not what other people are saying. It's not that "can't live on its own" or "lacking consciousness" alone makes an embryo different from a human or infant. There's massive difference between being incapable of those things in the present state because an individual lacks or has lost the ability vs because they are not at a developmental stage to biologically even have that capacity. These are different things and the point being made is to highlight how early stage this form of life is within its development not to apply singular binary metrics for whether or not to value life generally. 


And ultimately the statements here are not about any single thing but about applying them in aggregate. A dead body lacks a heartbeat as perhaps the most significant component. Yet a person suffering medical emergency without a heart beat for some duration isn't immediately a corpse - even when it could be minutes without a heart beat. We apply additional standards which are all evaluated in aggregate to determine if an individual currently exists or is a deceased corpse and the same goes for determination whether there is an individual living being at the other end during formation.


Now personally I think there is value in human life form that isn't yet an individual living being. So I think abortion after ~15-20 weeks is too late. But that's not on the basis that there is a singular living being already but that it's now too close to that point. If others argue they see that point earlier that I can understand and feel there's a meaningful discussion to be had - one on agreed upon relative terms. This insistence that there is meaningful independent life form automatically deserving the rights and legal protections we apply to individual persons is where it just becomes ridiculous and disingenuous for me. That absolutist, concrete statement utilizes a standard for life, development, state of being, and life stage term applicability which is not realistically applied by these people in any context besides this one.

It becomes a conversation that can have no end because the starting point is an embryo is an individual living human being (a person) vs an embryo is a human embryo - the earliest stage of human development obviously therefor coming much prior to being an individual living human being (a person). That latter starting point to me is concrete based on our shared universal perception of it in every context but this one. Again eggs are not birds or snakes - but eggs. So then having to operate under an entirely different premise here just makes the whole argument based in a reality that I don't think exists - one where we view the early stage reproductive replications of organisms as equivalent to their matured equivalents.

I think you are operating under a misconception, at least as regards American Conservatism. The point of Conservatism is not to stymie or slow down social and cultural developments just for the sake of it. The point isn’t to oppose change wholesale. We recognize that change is inevitable. Time only goes in one direction, and the future is coming whether you like it or not. Rather, the point is to preserve certain core principles into the future. Sometimes that will mean making adjustments, sometimes that will mean resisting certain changes. But the aim is always to preserve, or one might say to *conserve*, those principles. In the American context, the principles in question would probably best be summed up as Life, Liberty, and the Pursuit of Happiness.

As for technology, I am unaware of any technological advances to date that have left us worse off. Everywhere I look, the ultimate result of technological advancement has been greater prosperity, and greater potential for prosperity, for mankind. We lead a far more prosperous existence today than our forebears, and that can be soundly credited to technological innovation and capitalism. 

If a particular technology generally does present some threat, then obviously measures should be taken to mitigate the risks. That is just common sense. But adopting a blanket policy of ‘hitting the brakes’ on technological development out of blind fear seems to me rather irrational and likely to do us more harm than good.

I went looking for some examples of corporate bailouts in recent years. The only ones im finding are for farmers and the 2008 mortgage crisis. If you have any better sources or specific examples I cant find id love to review them.

Because of that I'll be splitting this discussion into two parts.

1. Previous bailouts.

A. Farmers.

Farmers operate with a profit margin of around 5% if i remember correctly. Meaning that they are property rich but cash poor. One of the biggest challenges facing farmers is that fewer people want to be farmers. So any lost business may be gone forever and create permanent damage. In addition, due to knock on effects it can damage other businesses that grow different crops.

B. 2008 mortgage crisis. 

This is where I can find the term too big to fail used in a non-propagandistic context. The issue here was how banks generate money. They dont actually have big vaults of cash. Instead they take your money and loan it out to other people. This is fine as long as the value keeps going up like with a house because if someone defaults the bank gets a $250K house for $150K. But what happens when that $250K house is suddenly worth only $50K? The bank now loses your money and has to make a federal insurance claim with the treasury. They were too big to fail because if the government did not step in we would have had another great depression. 

Im very light on the details intentionally because this is a reddit post. There was a lot of legal fuckery involved with subprime mortgages and the government put a lot more regulation on who these banks could loan to as a result. It was not a single bank but an entire industry that was too big to fail.

2. Future.

Lets assume for a minute that we have a company that is too big to fail. We'll call it Widget Corp. In order for it to be too big to fail we are talking about a company that has over 20% of the US GDP. For comparison, Amazon and Google are sitting around 2%. (I did the math a few months back for a different post. Thats about where they are but I can't remember the exact numbers.) 

In this case I would say that corporate bailouts should take the form of a federal investment scheme where the government buys stock equal to the bailout amount. If the stock isnt worth the bailout then it should go bankrupt.

3. Questions unanswered.

Both. In an ideal world, the government would not fund research or even education. Realistically, that isn't feasible, and stuff like national defense is a legitimate reason to expend public resources on, and maybe energy production, stuff that protects the commons. 

>If so, would you support the federal government getting almost entirely out of the business of funding basic scientific research?

Yes, absolutely. I support the government being limited to a narrow reading of the enumerated powers. 

>I'm asking because virtually every modern industrial power—including the United States, Germany, Japan, South Korea, the UK, and China—has historically invested heavily in basic research through public institutions.

Indeed, and when that is lead by the researchers, that is tolerable and decentralized, but still not good. There is a reason most of the best research is done from high name private institutes, and traditionally, the government has gone to these bodies and recruited when it perceived an application for their research. 

>One reason often given is that basic science frequently doesn't produce a predictable short-term commercial return. A private investor can justify funding a product that might reach the market in a few years, but it's much harder to justify funding a project that might not produce practical results for decades, if ever.

That is the modern belief, but its simply not true historically. Private investors are often more able to perceive both the long and short term potential benefits of research, and traditionally this is how funding was achieved, up until the 1930s when fiduciary responsibility was imposed on all corporations via the Supreme Court. Even today, most of the research for stuff like solar energy was done by privately funded research, ironically by gas companies who wanted to be prepared for peak oil back in the 60s and 70s. 

Additionally, its a self fulfilling prophecy, because why should private investors spend their own money when they can lobby the state to spend the people's money on it instead?

>Yet many of the technologies that underpin modern economies came from exactly that sort of research. The internet, GPS, mRNA technologies, semiconductor advances, nuclear power, and countless medical breakthroughs all had substantial public-sector involvement at various stages

And yet none of them were completely reliant on it, nor would have progressed to the point they are without massive private support and research. DARPANET only became the internet because it connected private groups together, and then was privatized. 

So the answer as to what replaces it is simple, private funding.

There are systems in which capitalism is more effective than socialism. The self-hating Westerners are part of the reason immigration can become problematic. In a healthy society, a certain level of immigration can be accommodated because those immigrant populations will eventually be assimilated into the native culture. Those parts of the immigrant culture which are compatible and valuable can be preserved and absorbed into the native culture, while aspect which are not compatible or valuable can be rejected. 

However, if the native population ceases to believe that its own culture is valuable, or, even worse, if people come to believe that their own culture is fundamentally evil and *shouldn’t* be preserved, then the mechanisms of assimilation will not operate, and eventually they will reverse until the native culture is replaced by or assimilated into the new dominant culture.

For a historical example of this sort of process, you just have to look at the rise of Christianity in the Roman Empire. Christianity didn’t conquer Rome by the sword. It became the dominant religion in the Roman world by a slow process of conversion. The Roman people themselves gradually ceased to believe in their own gods, and eventually there was no one left who even remembered a time when the Pantheon was anything other than a Christian church.

The problem in this for America is that politics is downstream of culture, as the famous saying goes. Laws are not self-enforcing, nor is the Constitution. In order for the freedoms guaranteed by our Constitution to be upheld, it is necessary for people to believe in the fundamental principles undergirding the Constitution. Those principles are themselves largely products of the Western cultural tradition, and they rely on Western assumptions for their support. If you lose Western culture, then so too goes the Liberty which most of us prize so dearly.

We are told to examine George Floyd's history.

We are told to examine the jury's history.

Fine.

Then why do so many conservatives refuse to examine Derek Chauvin's history?

Not the video.

Not the trial.

His history.

Because Chauvin did not appear out of nowhere on May 25, 2020.

Over his career with the Minneapolis Police Department, Chauvin accumulated approximately 17–18 misconduct complaints.

Most were dismissed or resulted in little discipline.

He received reprimands for misconduct.

He was involved in four shootings or fatal encounters.

One of those incidents resulted in the death of Wayne Reyes in 2006.

He was repeatedly accused of excessive force.

He was repeatedly accused of using unnecessary force during arrests.

He was repeatedly accused of escalating situations that were already under control.

He was named in multiple civil lawsuits.

In 2017, he restrained a 14-year-old child.

According to the federal case, Chauvin struck the child in the head with a flashlight, grabbed the child by the throat, handcuffed him, placed him face-down, and held his knee across the child's neck, shoulders, and upper back for approximately 15–16 minutes.

Years later, Chauvin admitted in federal court that this was unreasonable force and a violation of that child's civil rights.

That is not an allegation.

That is an admission.

The city of Minneapolis later paid nearly $9 million to settle lawsuits brought by John Pope Jr. and Zoya Code, who alleged Chauvin used similar excessive force against them before George Floyd was killed.

Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey later stated that Chauvin should have been fired after the 2017 incident.

During the trial, Chauvin's own profession turned on him.

Police Chief Medaria Arradondo testified that Chauvin's actions violated department policy, ethics, values, and training.

The department's use-of-force instructors testified that the restraint was not consistent with training.

The department's longest-serving officer testified that once a suspect is handcuffed and under control, the threat level drops dramatically and continued force is unnecessary.

Multiple officers and supervisors testified against Chauvin's conduct.

Then there is the larger system.

For years, Minneapolis struggled with a disciplinary process where arbitration frequently overturned or reduced officer discipline.

Police unions routinely challenged discipline and termination decisions.

Many complaints never resulted in meaningful consequences.

And despite years of complaints, lawsuits, force allegations, shootings, fatal encounters, warning signs, and eventually admitted civil-rights violations, Chauvin remained on patrol.

So if history matters, why doesn't his?

Because Chauvin wasn't just another officer.

He accumulated roughly 18 complaints over a 19-year career. That's nearly one complaint per year, substantially higher than the complaint rate for the average Minneapolis officer.

He was involved in four shootings or fatal encounters before 2020.

And while conservatives often treat that as normal police work, it isn't. Nationally, only about 27% of police officers ever fire their service weapon during their entire career. Roughly three-quarters of officers never do.

Yet Chauvin was involved in multiple shootings or fatal-force incidents, multiple excessive-force allegations, multiple lawsuits, multiple complaints, and later admitted to violating a child's civil rights through unreasonable force.

That is not the profile of an average officer.

That is the profile of an officer who generated warning signs for years.

If we're going to judge people by their history, then all histories should matter equally.

Including the history of the officer.

So why does everyone elses past matter, and not his?

The hypocrisy extends way beyond so-called family values. One of the more revealing contradictions within many modern authoritarian movements is their selective relationship with religion. Leaders often present themselves as defenders of faith, tradition, and religious values while simultaneously clashing with religious authorities whose teachings challenge their political objectives. This helps explain the strange spectacle of self-proclaimed defenders of Christianity attacking the Pope while expressing admiration for authoritarian figures such as Vladimir Putin or, in some cases, even Kim Jong Un.

The contradiction becomes easier to understand when viewed through the lens of power rather than theology. For many authoritarian movements, religion functions less as a source of moral guidance and more as a source of cultural identity and political legitimacy. The value of religion lies not in its ethical teachings but in its ability to reinforce group cohesion, traditional hierarchy, and loyalty. When religious leaders emphasize compassion, humility, care for migrants, concern for the poor, or moral constraints on political power, they may find themselves at odds with authoritarian movements despite sharing the same religious tradition.

This pattern is hardly new. Throughout history, authoritarian leaders have often embraced religion when it supports the state and marginalized it when it challenges the state. The issue is not faith itself but whether faith remains independent. An independent religious institution represents an alternative source of moral authority, and authoritarian movements are often uncomfortable with competing authorities. Loyalty is expected to flow toward the leader, the movement, or the nation first.

The admiration some authoritarians express toward strongmen such as Putin reflects a similar dynamic. What attracts them is often not the ideology of the foreign leader but the perception of strength, decisiveness, hierarchy, and centralized authority. The strongman appears unconstrained by criticism, opposition, courts, media scrutiny, or democratic compromise. In this sense, the appeal has less to do with religion or political philosophy and more to do with a recurring authoritarian attraction to certainty, order, and concentrated power. Once loyalty to strength becomes more important than adherence to consistent moral principles, contradictions that would normally seem obvious often cease to matter.

No, government doesn't automatically "work for everyone" just because it's a representative democracy. That's the naive assumption of a lot on the far left, funnily enough. In practice government works for the loudest, best-organized, and most connected interests and usually often at the expense of the quiet, independent, or anti-government citizen. Your logging uncle in a cabin in the woods working a 9-5 and reading old books still gets taxed at gunpoint for a government he explicitly doesn't want. He has no real opt-out. That's the fundamental difference.

On monopolies/utilities/ISPs: Yes, some sectors have limited competition and high barriers- these businesses are expensive to own and manage; the key is that "not practical for most people to go off-grid" doesn't turn a voluntary transaction into coercion. You can choose different providers where they exist, pressure for deregulation to increase competition, or yes you absolutely *can* live more simply. The IRS doesn't give you those options. They show up with force if you reject their "service." That's the distinction between state power and market power. Try to think about this honestly, please.

The core conservative principle is this: *coercion* requires strict limits and constant skepticism. Government has a monopoly on legitimate violence. Corporations do not have this power or ability in any fashion, internet hyperbole notwithstanding. I'll take a hundred greedy businesses I can ignore, boycott, or compete with over one unaccountable bureaucracy that can fine, regulate, or imprison me.

That's reality in a massive $6+ trillion government and a world where freedom of assembly and speech are valued: more money definitionally means more speech and more access. The honest fix isn't pretending "democracy" magically purifies this: democratic socialism is just *voting* to increase the size and scope of a deadly cancer instead of it just getting bigger organically. Neither one is good.

The fix is and always has been shrinking the prize worth capturing. Reduce the scope, power, and budget of the federal government so there's less for any elites whether wealthy, culturally empowered, media-boosted, or otherwise to hijack and utilize. Problem solved.
